posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked:
I admit to being a bit concerned after seeing this end up at 14.5% ABV and a couple of other reports from fellow Berserkers but my fears are assuaged.

It is what it is: a hot vintage of a great terroir made by talented winemakers. Blue and purple fruits, with a touch of gravel, a meaty savouriness, and a “high-toned” quality to the acid that keeps it fresh and vibrant. Silky texture, present but unobtrusive tannins. It’s definitely more Grenache than Pinot Noir on the Gamay expression scale that William Kelley and others here have detailed (Gamache?) but I don’t think it’s flabby/jammy/extracted/hot at all. The 2016 is still the gold standard for me but I wouldn’t kick this one outta bed for eating crackers, even if it may not be the longest-lived version of this cuvée.

I decided to pop this because the SO made delicious ribs with a sugary rub. I took a chance that it would work out and it ended up being a good marriage, with the wine cutting through the fatty pork and having enough body to run alongside.

I’m from a part of the American South where they don’t use any sugar in a pork rub. My post was about sugar in a pork rub. That’s a no-no. The sugar burns over the course of the cook. Some folks put it in the mop or the sauce. I’d debate them about that. Sean responded with what I take to be a joke about orthodox jewish law. It’s not something I would joke about regardless, but it has nothing to do with what I said and it’s only Sean that drew the connection to Judaism and my post and thought it appropriate to make a joke about it.

I tried the 2017 a year ago and found it very enjoyable at 13.5% ABV, albeit slightly chilled as it should be. 14.5% feels like such a jump that I’ll happily leave this vintage to others.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
I tried the 2017 a year ago and found it very enjoyable at 13.5% ABV, albeit slightly chilled as it should be. 14.5% feels like such a jump that I’ll happily leave this vintage to others.

Having had the wine once, I’ll happily leave all the remaining bottles of this vintage to others.

Sure, it’s a quite balanced and tasty effort - for a Grenache. However, as I’m not much of a Southern Rhône drinker, this wine really does nothing for me. Especially when I’m looking for a good, classic Bojo. No matter what others say, I found the wine so boozy upon tasting it I needed to grab the bottle to check out the alc% - and was floored to find the wine clocking at 14,5%. Well, it certainly tastes like that.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Hey Otto, assuming you’ve had more than just Foillard from 2018, is this common with all 2018 Bojos or just this producer? I normally quite like Foillard but haven’t had any of their 2018s.

I’ve had Foillard’s Cuvée Corcelette and Côte du Py along with a small handful of other 2018 Bojos.

The only fresh, classic Bojo I’ve had from 2018 has been Alex Foillard’s Brouilly, clocking at 13%.

In another thread William Kelley was saying some positive things about Guy Breton 2018’s as well.

Other than that, it seems like it’s a minefield I’ll gladly avoid. Apart from the Alex Foillard wine, all of them have been more Grenache than Gamay. Super-ripe, sweet-toned, relatively low in acidity and high in alcohol. People who have cut their teeth with Napa Cabs might still find them quite fresh and delicate, but they are just too clumsy for my palate.

I tend to drink my Beaujolais a wee bit cooler than most other reds. Had a killer 2016 Lapierre Morgon Sans Soufre last night, pulled from the wine fridge at 57 and put it in real fridge for maybe ten minutes. I’m thinking 53-55 is ideal. But the good news is, if you over-chill it, it warms in the glass, yay.

posts.name: John Kight
posts.cooked:
I tend to drink my Beaujolais a wee bit cooler than most other reds. Had a killer 2016 Lapierre Morgon Sans Soufre last night, pulled from the wine fridge at 57 and put it in real fridge for maybe ten minutes. I’m thinking 53-55 is ideal. But the good news is, if you over-chill it, it warms in the glass, yay.

I tend to do this with Beaujolais that I open and don’t love–I stick them in the fridge for a bit more chill. But my favorites (including Foillard, Thevenet and Lapierre) I prefer closer to 60, so I just pour at cellar temp and drink from there.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William Kelly loves Roilette in 2018 as well.

I haven’t tried any 18s yet, but I did just buy a sampler pack based mostly on WK’s praise for these:

2018 Clos de la Roilette Fleurie Cuvée Tardive
2018 Château Thivin Côte de Brouilly
2018 Thivin Reverdon
2018 Jean Foillard Morgon Côte du Py
2018 Diochon Moulin-á-Vent VV

I think you’ll enjoy all of those! Roilette Tardive 2018 is very reminiscent of the 2011…

To me, the hating on 2018 Beaujolais is a little exaggerated. Taste 500+ examples of it, and you will find that it’s a charming, easygoing, fruit-driven vintage as a whole; with some out-of-balance monsters (though fewer than 2015), and some really excellent wines which are built to age. If it isn’t the amazing vintage that the region’s publicity body claimed (and as several commentators reiterated), by no means is it a train wreck.

It seems, however, that despite what happened in 2003, 2005, 2009, 2015, and 2017, it’s only with the 2018 vintage that folks are figuring out that what is happening in all European wine regions is happening in the Beaujolais, and that the vintages that used to be the norm are likely, in the future, to be the exceptions. Hopefully the kind of balance attained in the best 2019s will be a more appealing model of what a more sun-kissed future can hold than 2018 is proving to be for some of the people who haven’t appreciated Foillard’s 2018 here…

posts.cooked: Had a bottle of the '18 Charmes Eponym last night. It was ripe and fleshy, with spiced blood plum fruit and plenty of florals. It is plush in the mouth, with good depth and a finish carried by sweet tannins. A very good drink but a notch below the Py.

Yes, here’s the review -

More extravagantly fruit-driven and enveloping than its elder sibling [the 2017, also reviewed in that issue], the 2018 Saint-Amour wafts from the glass with notions of black cherries, mulberries and licorice. On the palate, it’s medium to full-bodied, velvety and supple, with ripe acids, melting tannins and considerable charm. While it may not make old bones, this Saint-Amour is already hard to resist. 90/100

Cautionary caveat: the last two bottles of the 2015 I tasted were getting a bit oxidative, having been quite delicious on release.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Cautionary caveat: the last two bottles of the 2015 I tasted were getting a bit oxidative, having been quite delicious on release.

This raises an interesting question for the Beaujolais neophyte — what’s the typical aging curve for bojo? I’m sure there are numerous variables at work in such a question, like vintage, producer, appellation, bottling, etc, but I don’t really have much of a sense on this except that it’s a comparatively early maturing wine.

Can a good producer from a strong year comfortably hold for 5 years? How about 10?

Does bojo ever need age and if yes, does 1-2 years in the cellar do the trick most of the time?

I’ve heard Roilette often needs some age to show best, but is that the exception which proves the rule?

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
This raises an interesting question for the Beaujolais neophyte — what’s the typical aging curve for bojo? I’m sure there are numerous variables at work in such a question, like vintage, producer, appellation, bottling, etc, but I don’t really have much of a sense on this except that it’s a comparatively early maturing wine.

Can a good producer from a strong year comfortably hold for 5 years? How about 10?

Does bojo ever need age and if yes, does 1-2 years in the cellar do the trick most of the time?

I’ve heard Roilette often needs some age to show best, but is that the exception which proves the rule?

Pat, like any region it’s variable by producer and vintage. I’m drinking a combination of '09, '10, and '12 right now (Foillard, Lapierre, Breton, Chignard, etc.), and find all of them more complex, suppler and more interesting at this stage. That’s not to say that they weren’t slurpable and delicious when young, just that I prefer a little age on most wines. You can drink good Cru Beau young if you like that stage, but it’s not a wine that won’t age well in good vintages. I finished my '08s a little while ago (except a mag or two), and they were all still lovely wines, and I’ve had Cru Beau at 20 years out from vintage that was still wonderful.

This sounds ideal, especially after having to wait 25+ years for the bordeaux in my cellar to get to where I prefer it. Thanks.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Cautionary caveat: the last two bottles of the 2015 I tasted were getting a bit oxidative, having been quite delicious on release.

This raises an interesting question for the Beaujolais neophyte — what’s the typical aging curve for bojo? I’m sure there are numerous variables at work in such a question, like vintage, producer, appellation, bottling, etc, but I don’t really have much of a sense on this except that it’s a comparatively early maturing wine.

Can a good producer from a strong year comfortably hold for 5 years? How about 10?

Does bojo ever need age and if yes, does 1-2 years in the cellar do the trick most of the time?

I’ve heard Roilette often needs some age to show best, but is that the exception which proves the rule?

If it is “traditionally” made (meaning foot trodden whole bunches, a two week+ maceration, followed by élevage of >12 months in used barrels, foudres or cement), and comes from well-farmed vines and a creditable vintage, the aging potential is considerable! I have drunk many cru Beaujolais from years such as '71, '66, '64, '61, '59, '55, '53, '49, '47, '45 etc that are still in fine form today. I even have some 1964 Beaujolais without cru or even “Villages” status that is still a delicious drink: all its tannins are long melted away but it’s aging on its balance.

However, that’s a big “if”, given the ubiquity of thermovinification for more commercial wines and sans soufre cuvées at the other end of the spectrum. Producers that I think produce cuvées that will enjoy 20-30 years aging (or rather “maturing”, i.e. they improve rather than simply surviving) would include Alain Coudert’s Clos de la Roilette, Louis-Claude Desvignes, Daniel Bouland, Joseph Chamonard (who actually systematically late release), Foillard (off vintages of the early 1990s are still drinking well from good cellars), Jules Desjourneys, J-M Burgaud, and Château Thivin. That’s not an exhaustive list but it’s a good start.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
I opened another bottle tonight based on this thread. This wine is awful. I suppose there is a place for it. That place would be the toilet. Over-hot Liqueur cherry meets candied raspberry confiture. It’s as if Robert Parker murdered Foillard halfway through the harvest and then just ran amok. People who quibble over whether a mosel auslese goldkap should be labeled as a mosel auslese goldkap * should stay as far away from this wolf in sheep’s clothing as they can. It’s nuclear Beaujolais. Mother Nature has her own special way fighting back.
Alex

Mother Nature has her own special way fighting back.
Alex

I’m shocked that we’re talking about the same wine, because this was just not my experience at all!

Yeah, truly. Your note and Jeremy’s above describe the wine I tasted, but I don’t recognize this…

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked: Well, although my TN was not as extreme as Alex’s, I found the wine quite heavy and ponderous with the alcohol coming across as rather boozy most of the time. I dubbed the wine as CdP-CdP (Châteauneuf-du-Pape-Côte-du-Py) for a good reason.

posts.name: Eric S n y d e r
posts.cooked:
Well, although my TN was not as extreme as Alex’s, I found the wine quite heavy and ponderous with the alcohol coming across as rather boozy most of the time. I dubbed the wine as CdP-CdP (Châteauneuf-du-Pape-Côte-du-Py) for a good reason.

Just curious… if the bottle were labeled CdP, would you have liked it?

In other words, was it truly not a good wine, or just not what you want or expect from cru Beaujolais?
posts.display_username: Eric S n y d e r

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Well, although my TN was not as extreme as Alex’s, I found the wine quite heavy and ponderous with the alcohol coming across as rather boozy most of the time. I dubbed the wine as CdP-CdP (Châteauneuf-du-Pape-Côte-du-Py) for a good reason.

Just curious… if the bottle were labeled CdP, would you have liked it?

In other words, was it truly not a good wine, or just not what you want or expect from cru Beaujolais?

For me it was both bad and unrepresentative. I had to spit it out (that is not an exaggeration). It’s possible that I would do the same with any 14.5% wine, and it’s also possible that part of my reaction was from expecting a certain profile based on the last 20 years of drinking this every vintage and getting something different this time. It’s clearly the vintage and I suppose one way to think about it is that this is what nature gave Foillard in 2018.
A

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked:
in 2013 the foillard eponym was 11.7%
who knows if such a wine will even be possible in the future?
i must say tho 14% chez foillard packs a lot more punch than say 14% at dutraive. i was shocked how elegant and etheral the 17 fleurie champagne was despite the elevated alcohol.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
i was shocked how elegant and etheral the 17 fleurie champagne was despite the elevated alcohol.

Me too… but then I have had folks write me to say they had train-wreck bottles.

Mileage definitely seems to vary—between palates, between bottles, and perhaps between bottlings—more in the Beaujolais than in any other region I review.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
in 2013 the foillard eponym was 11.7%
who knows if such a wine will even be possible in the future?

Let’s not count it out: I’d bet '16, '14, '13, '12, '10 were all below 12.5%. That still makes for one vintage in two this decade… and would we really willingly swap the super-ripe vintages for the rotten, underripe years of yesteryear? As a niche of consumers we might; as producers we certainly wouldn’t.

The real issue in the Beaujolais will be whether producers can afford to make the viticultural adaptations that would mitigate some of the effects of a warming climate.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I opened another bottle tonight based on this thread. This wine is awful. I suppose there is a place for it. That place would be the toilet. Over-hot Liqueur cherry meets candied raspberry confiture. It’s as if Robert Parker murdered Foillard halfway through the harvest and then just ran amok. People who quibble over whether a mosel auslese goldkap should be labeled as a mosel auslese goldkap * should stay as far away from this wolf in sheep’s clothing as they can. It’s nuclear Beaujolais. Mother Nature has her own special way fighting back.
Alex

I’m shocked that we’re talking about the same wine, because this was just not my experience at all!

I’m not taken aback by Gillette’s and Otto’s comments. I felt similarly about the 2018 Bouland Delys cuvée, the flagship wine that I buy almost every year. Sometimes it just takes a minuscule amount extra to break the elastic, and once it does, it’s done for you. I do not recall finishing the Delys. The 2018 vintage is a big one. I did, however, really really like the Roilette Cuvee Tardive.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
The real issue in the Beaujolais will be whether producers can afford to make the viticultural adaptations that would mitigate some of the effects of a warming climate.

What are the viticultural adaptations that can mitigate some of the effects of climate change? Plant Syrah? Move to mars? Seems to me that if you can sell the wines at 14.5% alcohol you just play out the string as long as you can and leave the rest to your heirs.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
It’s clearly the vintage and I suppose one way to think about it is that this is what nature gave Foillard in 2018.
A

While I understand the variability of vintages, for sure, I think that vintners can make choices and not make monstrous wines, even in riper vintages. Nature may have leaned the wine a certain way, but somewhat balanced wines can be made in very ripe vintages. I haven’t had this particular wine, but I’m pretty experienced with Foillard and this isn’t normal. The '09, for example is a bigger wine, and while it isn’t my ideal Foillard, it isn’t something that I’d consider “undrinkable” or anything close to that. So I’m surprised by the comments on the '18.

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked:
I’m not taken aback by Gillette’s and Otto’s comments. I felt similarly about the 2018 Bouland Delys cuvée, the flagship wine that I buy almost every year. Sometimes it just takes a minuscule amount extra to break the elastic, and once it does, it’s done for you. I do not recall finishing the Delys. The 2018 vintage is a big one. I did, however, really really like the Roilette Cuvee Tardive.

I would understand a stylistic preference of classically crunchy, bright red fruit over this more rounded darker profile, but to call this undrinkably extracted and Parkerized - or even Châteauneuf! - just feels like we’re drinking two different wines. Like I said, I felt it was more than recognizably Gamay and it wouldn’t be my favoured expression of this cuvée but I still enjoyed it.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Well, although my TN was not as extreme as Alex’s, I found the wine quite heavy and ponderous with the alcohol coming across as rather boozy most of the time. I dubbed the wine as CdP-CdP (Châteauneuf-du-Pape-Côte-du-Py) for a good reason.

Just curious… if the bottle were labeled CdP, would you have liked it?

In other words, was it truly not a good wine, or just not what you want or expect from cru Beaujolais?

A great question! Perhaps I would’ve considered a surprisingly great Châteauneuf, since it would’ve shown relatively high acidity for one and although quite brooding and plummy in character, the taste would’ve still been showing more berry-fruit flavors than your typical Châteauneuf would have.

But no. I still would’ve not liked it, since I don’t like Châteauneuf-du-Pape either. I struggle to enjoy lush, super-ripe wines and when alcohol in a wine is so high it starts to taste boozy, it’s more of a flaw than a feature in a wine.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
It’s clearly the vintage and I suppose one way to think about it is that this is what nature gave Foillard in 2018.
A

While I understand the variability of vintages, for sure, I think that vintners can make choices and not make monstrous wines, even in riper vintages. Nature may have leaned the wine a certain way, but somewhat balanced wines can be made in very ripe vintages. I haven’t had this particular wine, but I’m pretty experienced with Foillard and this isn’t normal. The '09, for example is a bigger wine, and while it isn’t my ideal Foillard, it isn’t something that I’d consider “undrinkable” or anything close to that. So I’m surprised by the comments on the '18.

I like the 2009 Foillard and still have a couple bottles although I don’t think it has gotten any better with age. It comes in at 13% alcohol if I remember correctly, which is a huge difference from the 2019.
A

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It’s clearly the vintage and I suppose one way to think about it is that this is what nature gave Foillard in 2018.
A

While I understand the variability of vintages, for sure, I think that vintners can make choices and not make monstrous wines, even in riper vintages. Nature may have leaned the wine a certain way, but somewhat balanced wines can be made in very ripe vintages. I haven’t had this particular wine, but I’m pretty experienced with Foillard and this isn’t normal. The '09, for example is a bigger wine, and while it isn’t my ideal Foillard, it isn’t something that I’d consider “undrinkable” or anything close to that. So I’m surprised by the comments on the '18.

I like the 2009 Foillard and still have a couple bottles although I don’t think it has gotten any better with age. It comes in at 13% alcohol if I remember correctly, which is a huge difference from the 2019.
A

The 2009 is actually 14%, even though it is labelled at 13%. This is what my predecessor, David Shildknecht, wrote about it in 2011:

Foillard’s 2009 Morgon Cote du Py – bottled last September – displays game and cherry with kirsch distillate and smoky black tea overtones. Not in the least heavy or confitured, it evinces unusual – indeed, uncanny brightness and lift for its vintage, finishing long and lusciously, with alluring spice and saliva-stimulating salinity. “Maybe too rich,” says Foillard – knitting his brow – about this 14% alcohol wine. But that thought would not have crossed my mind. This beauty ought to perform well for at least half a dozen years.

The fact that the 2018 is honestly labelled might have something to do with tariffs…

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: that has crossed my mind as well regarding the tariffs, so it would be interesting to get some samples analyzed. i have drank a fair bit of 09 from foillard, especially the 3.14 and while ripe it still tasted of gamay. the 15 vintage was the first time i thought the wines were over the top.

I’ve got like a whole case of this coming, and I’m pretty concerned I’ve overbought. I enjoyed the 2015 in a ripe year, but it’s a little tiring and other than that one wine I drink pretty much NOTHING at these levels. Ripe years happen, but I’ve entertained the thought, not singling Foillard in particular at all – they’re my favorite producer – but generally with gamay sorta having its day, there’s an incentive across bojo to puff things up and cash in. Is that terrible?

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked: Apparently, a certain cat in this household aligns with the naysayers on this wine.

posts.name: Johan R I C K F O R S
posts.cooked:
Had to concur more with the critics than with the more positive notes:

I DO NOT LIKE THIS WINE:
88 points
5 september 2020 - I’m surprised by the many very positive notes I am seeing. Foillard as usual has delivered a well made wine in this vintage, with signatures as gravelly minerality, violet and satin texture all there. They match up with cherry, strawberry and raspberry. But the acidity is not just there in a sufficient way, the structure is more about the stemmy tannins and I feel that the alcohol peeks through. I was happy to have given this a shot also in this vintage because Foillard is such a favourite that it deserves this, but also happy to wait for the next vintage and drink my older ones until then. 2014 and 2016 are obviously far superior but also the 2017 I liked a lot more.
posts.display_username: Johan R I C K F O R S

posts.cooked:
Find this entire thread fascinating given the seemingly totally different impressions of this wine. I’ve personally had really mixed experiences with 2018 Beaujolais even from producers I usually like (particularly did not find anything redeeming in a couple of the Julien Sunier 2018s). I drank the 2018 Foillard villages a couple weeks a go and loved it. For whatever reason that wine was made in the much lighter, bright red fruit style that I think a lot of us love in Gamay (believe ABV was like 12.5%). Given my positive experience with that and my admiration for Foillard’s wines in general I ordered a sampling of the other 2018 cuvees and tried the Cote du Py last night.

I have to say I fall in the same camp as Sean/WK and found this wine incredibly enjoyable. As I said above I’ve had some really negative experiences with 18 bojo and 18 reds from europe in general and I consider myself highly sensitive to out of balance or highly alcoholic wines. With all that in mind I really didn’t find anything to dislike about this wine. I definitely agree it shows a little more the “grenache side” of gamay but it’s still gamay and I’ve essentially never had a grenache based wine I liked and I still loved this wine (At times it did remind me of the Pedres Blanques which I think is the only grenache I’ve ever liked). Yes it’s a little darker and slightly riper (there is a little grapeyness that creeps in here and there but again I hate grapey wines and I did not find this to be a significant issue with this wine) but it’s still incredibly fresh with lifted aromatics and plenty of acidity. I agree with William that it’s hard to reconcile the wine I tasted with some of the other notes here and probably just speaks to some combination of issues with bottle variation but also that while we all experience wine similarly enough to share our thoughts with each other there is enough real difference in sensual experience to frustrate our communication around wine.

posts.name: Napoleon G
posts.cooked:

I had the 2018 Foillard Village around the same time and immediately went back to buy more. Have you had a chance to try his standard 2018 Morgon “Classique?” I bought some of these after being impressed with the Village, and am curious how it shows compared to the Cote Du Py/Village level wines.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William Kelly loves Roilette in 2018 as well.

I haven’t tried any 18s yet, but I did just buy a sampler pack based mostly on WK’s praise for these:

2018 Clos de la Roilette Fleurie Cuvée Tardive
2018 Château Thivin Côte de Brouilly
2018 Thivin Reverdon
2018 Jean Foillard Morgon Côte du Py
2018 Diochon Moulin-á-Vent VV

I think you’ll enjoy all of those! Roilette Tardive 2018 is very reminiscent of the 2011…

To me, the hating on 2018 Beaujolais is a little exaggerated. Taste 500+ examples of it, and you will find that it’s a charming, easygoing, fruit-driven vintage as a whole; with some out-of-balance monsters (though fewer than 2015), and some really excellent wines which are built to age. If it isn’t the amazing vintage that the region’s publicity body claimed (and as several commentators reiterated), by no means is it a train wreck.

It seems, however, that despite what happened in 2003, 2005, 2009, 2015, and 2017, it’s only with the 2018 vintage that folks are figuring out that what is happening in all European wine regions is happening in the Beaujolais, and that the vintages that used to be the norm are likely, in the future, to be the exceptions. Hopefully the kind of balance attained in the best 2019s will be a more appealing model of what a more sun-kissed future can hold than 2018 is proving to be for some of the people who haven’t appreciated Foillard’s 2018 here…

Drinking that 2018 Roilette right now, I’m not so sold. Could use more cut and precision. One of the most forward Cuvee Tardives that I recall. Very primary as well, but I guess that’s to be expected. I’ll stick these away to allow them, hopefully, to morph. I just picked up last week a 6-pack of the Griffe du Marquis. Thoughts on the drinking window?

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: We drank a magnum of 2018 Foillard Cote du Py over Labor Day weekend and found it pretty much exactly as William said - fruit forward a d sun-kissed, exuberant, but not overblown or stewed or oppressive. It was very easy to enjoy with friends and food and no expectations of the sublime.

posts.cooked:

You can try one young, I think…

I’d be interested to see your impressions of a blind tasting of the last ten years of Tardive, incidentally. But if you’re struggling with the 2018, you should probably steer clear of the 2020.

Harvest this year in the Beaujolais looks like beginning around Sept. 15 in Moulin-à-Vent, by the way, so potentially a throw-back vintage along the lines of 2016 or 2014 (but so far without serious hail issues). I may make a couple of barrels myself just to try to understand things from the inside out a bit better.

But I’m looking forward to ‘19 Griffe arriving soon!

Will definitely grab the ‘19. Thanks, William, with the warning on ‘20. And if you wanna do that 10+ year blind, I’ve got the vertical on both cuvees.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: There’s nothing wrong per se with the '18 Griffe, but no, it’s not the strongest year for the wine. I found it a bit hollow, but it’s not hot or roasted. There is no harm in popping one now. I have a few and I’m not sorry to have them, but I’m more excited for '19.

Found a source: 80-year-old vines in the best part of Champ de Cour. Could be interesting. I have certainly found that vinifying some Pinot Noir has taught me more about Burgundy than anything else, so this might provide some insights into the Beaujolais. It’s also inexpensive enough to be a pretty economical “house wine”.

links.title: French naturalistas and other curiosities (Foillard, Dauvissat, Desjourneys, Vini Viti Vinci, etc.) - Wine Berserkers - international wine social media, online community, and discussion
